intro:
Protein is the most important macronutrient for building muscle, recovering from exercise, supporting immune function, and maintaining metabolic health. Yet most people either consume too little (especially if active) or are confused about how much they actually need based on their goals and activity level.

Our protein intake calculator uses evidence-based multipliers from sports nutrition research to give you a personalized daily protein target based on your body weight, activity level, and goal (general health, weight loss, muscle building, or performance). Results are shown as a range and a recommended target, plus a per-meal guide for practical planning.

Protein needs range from 0.8 g/kg (sedentary adults, minimum requirement) to 2.2 g/kg or more (elite athletes in heavy training). Most active people benefit from 1.2–2.0 g/kg per day.
howItWorks: Enter your body weight in kilograms, select your activity level, and choose your primary goal. The calculator applies research-based multipliers to give you a minimum, maximum, and recommended daily protein intake in grams, plus how to distribute that across 3–4 meals per day.
interpretationGuide: The recommended grams per day is your daily target. The range shows the minimum needed (lower) to a level supporting maximum muscle protein synthesis (upper). Spreading protein across 3–4 meals of 25–40 grams each maximizes muscle building compared to eating the same total in one or two large servings.

faqs.question: How much protein do I need to build muscle?
faqs.answer: Research consistently supports 1.6–2.2 g/kg of body weight per day for maximizing muscle protein synthesis in resistance-trained individuals. Below 1.6 g/kg, muscle gains are suboptimal. Going above 2.2 g/kg offers minimal additional benefit for most people.

faqs.question: What is the minimum protein intake for health?
faqs.answer: The US RDA for protein is 0.8 g/kg per day, sufficient to prevent deficiency in sedentary adults. However, for active people, older adults (who need 1.0–1.2 g/kg to preserve muscle), and anyone with fat loss or muscle gain goals, significantly more is beneficial.

faqs.question: Does eating more protein help with weight loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Protein has the highest thermic effect of food (20–30% of calories burned in digestion), increases satiety hormones, reduces hunger hormones, and preserves lean muscle during a calorie deficit. Most research recommends 1.2–1.6 g/kg for people in a calorie deficit.

faqs.question: How much protein can the body absorb in one meal?
faqs.answer: Older research suggested a 25–40 g ceiling per meal for muscle protein synthesis. More recent research suggests the body can use larger amounts but that spreading intake across 3–4 meals optimizes 24-hour muscle protein synthesis. Aim for 25–50g per meal.

faqs.question: What foods are highest in protein?
faqs.answer: Chicken breast (31g/100g), tuna (30g/100g), Greek yogurt (10g/100g), cottage cheese (11g/100g), eggs (13g/100g), lentils (9g/100g), tempeh (19g/100g), and whey protein powder (75–90g/100g). Animal proteins are generally more bioavailable than plant proteins.

faqs.question: Is too much protein harmful to kidneys?
faqs.answer: For people with healthy kidneys, high protein intake is safe. Multiple studies show no adverse effects in healthy adults up to 2.2 g/kg. People with pre-existing kidney disease should consult a doctor before significantly increasing protein intake.

faqs.question: What is leucine and why does it matter for protein?
faqs.answer: Leucine is a branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) that acts as a molecular trigger for muscle protein synthesis. Each meal should ideally contain 2–3 g of leucine to maximally stimulate muscle building. High-leucine foods include whey, milk, chicken, beef, eggs, and soybeans.

faqs.question: Are plant proteins as good as animal proteins?
faqs.answer: Animal proteins have higher bioavailability and complete amino acid profiles. Plant proteins (except soy) are often lower in one or more essential amino acids and have lower digestibility. Vegans and vegetarians should aim for the higher end of protein recommendations and combine diverse plant sources to get all essential amino acids.

faqs.question: When should I eat protein for optimal muscle building?
faqs.answer: Distribute protein evenly across 3–4 meals throughout the day. Having protein within 2 hours after resistance training appears beneficial, though the 'anabolic window' is broader than once believed. A leucine-rich protein serving before bed (slow-digesting casein or cottage cheese) may also support overnight muscle repair.

faqs.question: How do I convert my weight from pounds to kilograms for this calculator?
faqs.answer: Divide pounds by 2.205. Examples: 150 lbs ÷ 2.205 = 68 kg; 180 lbs ÷ 2.205 = 81.6 kg; 200 lbs ÷ 2.205 = 90.7 kg.
